claims.claim_text: McCormick is the global leader in spices, seasonings, and flavor with leading category shares spanning a broad range of flavor types and all price points. The brand commands 46% of US millennials' recognition as the 'best spice brand' with another 37% classifying it as 'one of the best', demonstrating strong multigenerational brand resonance.
evidence_quotes: As a global leader in flavor, McCormick is well positioned to meet this demand. We have leading category shares that span a broad range of flavors and all price points from premium to private label. (Q4 FY2013, Alan Wilson)

A recent study shows that 46% of U.S. millennials regard the McCormick brand as the best spice brand in the market, and another 37% categorized it as one of the best. Again, these metrics are similar to what we see with other age groups. (Q4 FY2014, Alan Wilson)
claims.notes: 83% of US millennials view McCormick as best or one of the best spice brands - strong brand data. Global leadership position with category leadership across multiple markets supports Branding power classification. Score 0.7.

claims.claim_text: McCormick's Comprehensive Continuous Improvement (CCI) program has generated $63M in cost savings in 2013 (vs $45M target), $65M in 2014, targeting $85M+ in 2015. This systematic cost reduction capability creates a self-funding growth engine that competitors at smaller scale cannot replicate to the same degree.
evidence_quotes: In 2013, cost savings from CCI reached $63 million, well above our initial projection of at least $45 million for the year. (Q4 FY2013, Alan Wilson)

Employees throughout McCormick delivered $65 million in cost savings from our Comprehensive Continuous Improvement program, CCI, and we have embarked on a more aggressive cost savings plan for 2015. (Q4 FY2014, Alan Wilson)
In 2015, we've set a goal to generate at least $85 million in cost savings, led by our CCI program. (Q4 FY2014, Alan Wilson)
claims.notes: CCI is a systematic process capability, not just a one-time cost cut. The ability to generate $65-85M annually in savings from a $4-5B revenue base (~1.3-1.7% of sales) reflects embedded Process Power. Score 0.5 - consistent savings demonstrate capability but industry peers also run similar programs.

claims.claim_text: McCormick's global flavoring network across both consumer and industrial segments (16 innovation centers in 12 countries) provides scale advantages in R&D, new product development, and cross-market innovation transfer. Successful innovations in one market (gluten-free gravy mixes from Canada, squeeze pouch from China) are systematically rolled out globally.
evidence_quotes: With 16 innovation centers in 12 countries around the world, we continue to invest in product development. (Q4 FY2014, Alan Wilson)

We've made the U.S. gourmet line, featuring greater variety, a flavor-seal technology that locks in flavor, color and aroma... In France, our Vahiné premium vanilla has driven category growth. (Q4 FY2014, Alan Wilson)

We grew sales in Canada with very successful innovation that included grilling sauces and marinades, gluten-free gravies, new honey packaging and a line of authentic Asian products... Introducing these in the U.S., such as our line of gluten-free gravy mixes. (Q4 FY2013, Alan Wilson)
claims.notes: 16 global innovation centers enable systematic cross-market innovation transfer. This is a Scale Economy: fixed R&D costs spread across larger global revenue base. Score 0.5.

claims.claim_text: McCormick's industrial business serves major food manufacturers and quick-service restaurant chains as a customized flavor solutions provider. These relationships involve proprietary flavor formulations designed into customers' products - creating high switching costs as reformulating products requires FDA approval, consumer testing, and supply chain restructuring.
evidence_quotes: With our foundation in spices and herbs, industrial customers continue to view McCormick as the go-to supplier to help grow their range of healthy and flavorful products. (Q4 FY2014, Alan Wilson)

Customers continue to turn to McCormick to improve the healthy attributes of their products, including more simple ingredients. In 2013, about 1/3 of our industrial new product briefs had some type of wellness aspect. (Q4 FY2013, Alan Wilson)

Flavor is well aligned with the broader changes in the way that people are eating today... This is a catalyst, not only to our consumer business, but also to our industrial business. (Q4 FY2014, Alan Wilson)
claims.notes: Industrial flavor formulations embedded in customers' products create real switching costs. However, management reports 'continued weakness in demand from quick service restaurants', suggesting these switching costs are not always strong enough to maintain volumes. Score 0.5.

claims.claim_text: The spice and seasoning category is projected to grow at a mid-single-digit rate globally in 2015 and beyond (Euromonitor data), driven by structural trends: demand for new flavors, ethnic cuisines, health-conscious eating (replacing sugar, salt, fat with spices). McCormick as category leader is positioned to benefit from this secular growth.
evidence_quotes: Spice and seasoning category growth was strong in 2014 and is projected to grow at a mid single-digit rate globally in 2015, with roughly 4% growth in developed markets and nearly a 10% increase in emerging markets. (Q4 FY2014, Alan Wilson)

Flavor is well aligned with the broader changes in the way that people are eating today, whether it's spicier food, eating more fresh produce and protein, simple ingredients or the move towards healthier eating. We think that these changes are here to stay and that each of them drives demand for flavor. (Q4 FY2014, Alan Wilson)
claims.notes: Category growth projections backed by Euromonitor data. Mid-single-digit global growth with 10% in emerging markets is a genuine secular tailwind. Score 0.5 because this benefits all category participants, not just McCormick.

claims.claim_text: McCormick's marketing investment has grown 64% over the past 5 years (2013 vs 2008) exceeding $200M for the first time in 2013, achieving ROIs above food industry averages. Digital marketing was doubled in 2 years (2011 to 2013) with digital planned at 1/3 of total advertising in 2015. This investment consistency builds brand equity moat against private label and smaller competitors.
evidence_quotes: Our marketing support exceeded $200 million for the first time in 2013. It was up 64% in the past 5 years. We continue to achieve returns on this spending that are ahead of food industry averages. (Q4 FY2013, Alan Wilson)
Digital marketing has a particularly high ROI, and we're making it a greater part of the mix. This year, it's planned at 1/3 of our total advertising, up from 11% in 2010. (Q4 FY2014, Alan Wilson)
claims.notes: 64% marketing increase over 5 years with above-average ROI is a Branding power investment pattern. However, management reports continuing share gains by private label and smaller competitors, suggesting the brand moat is being contested. Score 0.5.

key_themes: Global flavor leadership with 83% millennial brand recognition (Branding)
CCI program generating $65-85M annual cost savings (Scale/Process)
16 global innovation centers enabling cross-market product transfer
Industrial flavoring with embedded customer formulations (Switching Costs)
Mid-single-digit secular category growth tailwind globally
